title: End of module: Variable Acceleration — Calculus in Kinematics

questions.text: A particle's displacement is $s = 2t^3 - 5t^2 + 3t + 1$ (m). Find its velocity $v$ at $t = 2\,\text{s}$.
A particle's displacement is given by $s(t)$. Which expression gives its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Velocity is the first derivative of displacement, and acceleration is the derivative of velocity — i.e. the **second** derivative of displacement, $a = \dfrac{\mathrm{d}^2s}{\mathrm{d}t^2}$.

questions.text: A particle moves so that $s = t^4 - 4t^3$ (m). Find its acceleration $a$ at $t = 1\,\text{s}$.
A particle has velocity $v = 6t^2 - 18t + 12$ (m s$^{-1}$) for $t \ge 0$. At what time (in seconds) is the particle first instantaneously at rest? Give the smaller value of $t$.
questions.explanation: Set $v = 0$: $6t^2 - 18t + 12 = 0 \Rightarrow t^2 - 3t + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow (t-1)(t-2) = 0$, so $t = 1$ or $t = 2$. The smaller value is $t = 1\,\text{s}$.

questions.text: A particle has acceleration $a = 12t^2$ (m s$^{-2}$) and initial velocity $v(0) = 2\,\text{m s}^{-1}$. Find its velocity at $t = 3\,\text{s}$.
A particle has velocity $v = 4t - t^2$ (m s$^{-1}$) and starts at $s(0) = 2\,\text{m}$. Find its displacement at $t = 2\,\text{s}$. Give your answer as a decimal to 2 d.p.
A particle has velocity $v = t^2 - 6t + 5$ (m s$^{-1}$). At the time when the acceleration is zero, what is the value of $v$ (the minimum velocity)?
questions.explanation: Acceleration is $a = \dfrac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}t} = 2t - 6$. Setting $a = 0$ gives $t = 3$. Then $v(3) = 9 - 18 + 5 = -4\,\text{m s}^{-1}$, the minimum velocity (since the $t^2$ coefficient is positive, this is a minimum turning point of $v$).

questions.text: Given $a(t)$, which process finds $v(t)$, and what extra information is needed to find a specific numerical constant?
choices: Differentiate $a$; no extra information is needed
Integrate $a$; an initial condition (e.g. $v$ at a known time) is needed to fix the constant of integration
Integrate $a$ twice; no extra information is needed
Differentiate $a$ twice; an initial condition is needed
questions.explanation: Velocity is found by integrating acceleration, $v = \int a\,\mathrm{d}t$, which introduces an arbitrary constant of integration. An initial condition (such as $v$ at $t=0$) is required to determine that constant's numerical value.

questions.text: A particle has velocity $v = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$ (m s$^{-1}$) and starts at the origin, $s(0) = 0$. Find its displacement at $t = 1\,\text{s}$.
questions.explanation: Integrate: $s = \int (3t^2 - 12t + 9)\,\mathrm{d}t = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + D$, and $s(0)=0$ gives $D=0$. At $t=1$: $s = 1 - 6 + 9 = 4\,\text{m}$.

questions.text: A particle's velocity changes sign twice during a journey (it reverses direction twice). Which statement correctly compares its total distance travelled with its displacement over that journey?
choices: They must always be equal, whatever the motion
Total distance travelled is generally greater than or equal to the magnitude of the displacement, because reversing direction adds extra path length that partially cancels in the displacement
Displacement is always greater than distance travelled
Distance travelled cannot be found using calculus
questions.explanation: Displacement is the net change in position, $s(t_2)-s(t_1)$, while distance is the sum of the lengths of each leg of the journey between direction reversals (found by splitting the integral at each $t$ where $v=0$ and summing absolute values). Reversals mean some path length cancels out of the net displacement, so distance $\ge$ |displacement|, with equality only when the velocity never changes sign.
